data.title: TLTW: Investors Losing Confidence
data.description:
Recently, I wrote a bullish article on the Simplify Short Term Treasury Futures Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:TUA), arguing the TUA ETF was a positive-carry hedge against economic weakness. Some readers messaged me and asked why I did not recommend long bond funds like the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (BATS:TLTW).

In my opinion, President Trump's tariff policies may spur inflation, which could lead to a hawkish response from the Federal Reserve and may be a headwind for long-term bonds like TLTW.

More importantly, the current administration's policies may worsen the budget deficit of the U.S. government, increasing long-term default risk.

Finally, by antagonizing its largest trading partners, America may be reducing international demand for treasury securities.

Putting it all together, I believe it is much simpler and easier to bet on lower short-term interest rates with the TUA ETF rather than long-term interest rates with the TLTW ETF. I continue to rate TLTW a hold.

Brief Fund Overview

For those not familiar, the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond BuyWrite Strategy ETF ("TLTW") is a 'derivative income funds' that combine an option-writing strategy with an index fund that buys 20+ year treasuries. The TLTW ETF tracks the investment returns of holding the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) and writes one-month covered call options to generate income.

The TLTW has $1 billion in assets under management ("AUM") and charges investors a 0.35% expense ratio (Figure 1).

Are Bonds And Stocks Always Negatively Correlated?

Historically, when the economy and equity markets are weak, bonds tend to perform well, creating a negative correlation between equities and bonds (Figure 2). This negative correlation has been the basis for the "60/40" portfolio, where financial advisors recommend investors invest 40% of their portfolio in bonds and 60% in equities as one tends to offset the other.

The negative correlation between bonds and stocks can be partly explained by investors rushing into safe-haven assets like bonds, especially those backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government.

However, if we stretch the time series farther back, a negative correlation between stocks and bonds is a relatively recent phenomenon, as there have been periods in the 30s-50s and 70s-90s when the correlation between stocks and bonds was positive (Figure 3).

Inflation Is The Key Driver

In my opinion, the key driver for the correlation between stocks and bonds is inflation (Figure 4).

When inflation is high, central banks have less control of monetary policy as they need to maintain price stability by raising or keeping interest rates high. Higher interest rates cause bonds to lose value, even if stocks fall and the economy weakens.

We saw this effect briefly in 2022 when bonds and stocks fell in tandem, as the Federal Reserve was forced to raise interest rates to combat inflation (Figure 5 ).

However, as inflation moderated and the Federal Reserve slowed down its interest rate increases, stocks stabilized, and a semblance of the negative correlation between stocks and bonds returned.

Trump Tariffs Cause Market Chaos

In recent weeks, the biggest market development has been President Trump unilaterally announcing 'reciprocal tariffs' on almost every country on the planet. Although these 'reciprocal tariffs' were later paused, except for a 145% levy against Chinese imports and exemptions on smartphones and computer chips, stock markets around the globe have been hit hard by the tariff shock (Figure 6).

One surprise to market pundits has been the weakness in long-term treasury bonds. Instead of treasury yields falling (and bonds rising) as a sign of investors rushing into the safe haven asset, 10-year treasury yields surged higher by over 40 bps at the beginning of the week, causing steep losses to investors' bond portfolios (Figure 7).

In fact, one of the key reasons President Trump changed course regarding the 'reciprocal tariffs' was due to the breakdown in correlation between bonds and stocks.

Are Markets Losing Confidence?

However, even as President Trump softened the blow of his 'reciprocal tariffs', treasury yields resumed their ascent, with the US 30-year treasury resuming its climb to nearly 5% on April 10th (Figure 8).

In my opinion, there are two explanations for this rise in treasury yields. First, tariffs will likely increase the prices of goods, which could lead to higher inflation and a more hawkish response from the Federal Reserve. A recent gauge of consumer confidence suggests inflation remains top of mind for consumers.

More importantly, the rise in yields could be an indication of global investors potentially losing their confidence in the 'full faith and credit' of the United States. For example, if we look at the Credit Default Swap ("CDS") of the United States, it shows that U.S. CDS spreads rose to 42 bps, nearly the highest in the past 2 decades except for a brief period during the 2023 debt ceiling crisis and the 2008 Great Financial Crisis (Figure 9).

Trump Policies Expected To Widen The Deficit

There are many reasons why investors may be losing faith in the U.S. As I have written in the past, the point of President Trump and Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency's ("DOGE") $2 trillion in budget cuts appears to be to fund a continuation of President Trump's tax cuts that are expected to add $5.7 trillion to the national debt.

Putting aside the human cost of laying off tens of thousands of government employees, the DOGE 'savings' appear to be far short of Elon Musk's initial target, with the billionaire now touting $150 billion in savings instead of $2 trillion (Figure 10).

Assuming these savings are real (in the early days of the DOGE cuts, the DOGE team mistakenly claimed to have cut $8 billion in a government contract that turned out to be only $8 million), the cuts are but a drop in the bucket against the $5.7 trillion cost of the proposed tax cuts.

Furthermore, amidst the government chaos, the Internal Revenue Service ("IRS") estimates that tax revenues will decline by 10%, or $500 billion, as individuals and businesses may choose not to pay their taxes this year after DOGE cut tens of thousands of IRS auditors.

Also, since the IRS has been forced to share information with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement ("ICE"), the IRS will likely see a sharp drop in the nearly $100 billion in taxes that undocumented immigrants pay in taxes.

Putting it all together, in my opinion, the current administration's policies may worsen a budget deficit that was expected to be over 6% for the foreseeable future (Figure 11).

Antagonizing Buyers Of Treasuries

On top of a potentially worsening fiscal situation, the administration also appears to be antagonizing its global trading partners, who also happen to be the largest international buyers of treasuries. According to Treasury data, China is the second largest holder of treasuries with a $761 billion hoard (Figure 12).

However, with a 145% tariff on Chinese imports, the administration has effectively cut off trade with China, worth $460 billion in U.S. imports in 2024. With no US dollars to recycle, China may not need to continue buying U.S. treasuries. Similarly, other trading partners may see less need to buy U.S. treasuries if their trade with America declines due to Trump's tariffs.

Risk To Being Bearish Treasuries

Of course, one should not be too bearish on U.S. treasuries. While the U.S. CDS spread is higher, 42 bps is still a very low level of default risk. Furthermore, the Federal Reserve can always print an unlimited amount of money to buy treasury bonds, effectively limiting treasury yields at a certain point via yield curve control.

However, if this were to occur, investors should expect a dramatic decrease in the U.S. dollar's exchange rate against foreign currencies, as policymakers cannot control all three pillars of the Mundell-Fleming trilemma (Figure 13).

Conclusion

Trump's tariffs threaten to increase inflation, which could cause the Federal Reserve to maintain a more hawkish stance on monetary policy and may act as a headwind for bonds.

With President Trump pushing for a major tax cut, I believe the U.S. budget deficit may get worse in the coming years, despite DOGE's best efforts to cut government spending. This is a long-term negative for treasury bonds.

Furthermore, the administration appears to be antagonizing its trading partners like China who happen to be major buyers of treasury securities. With reduced trade, there is likely less demand for treasuries.

Putting it all together, I believe it is much safer to bet on a lowering of short-term interest rates due to a worsening economy instead of long-term interest rates with the TLTW ETF. At the short-end, the Federal Reserve has control via the Fed Funds rate. I continue to maintain a cautious stance on TLTW and rate it a hold.

data.title: Valeura Energy: Margin Of Safety, But Falling Prices Are A Concern
data.description:
Canadian exploration and production ("E&P") company, Valeura Energy Inc. (OTCQX:VLERF), has entered into a period of extreme uncertainty. In the year-to-date ("YTD"), the price is down 8.3%, and just yesterday, the price fell nearly 14%. Crude oil prices have fallen in the last year, from $87.24 per barrel to $61.05 per barrel, which does not bode well for the profitability of oil firms. The market does not seem to believe that Valeura will be profitable in a world of tariffs.

The Business Model

Valeura’s core business model relies on its ability to buy, develop and efficiently produce conventional hydrocarbon reserves in Thailand and Türkiye. The company is also pursuing inorganic growth opportunities in Southeast Asia. There are three key elements to this business model: the asset portfolio, operational efficiency and growth through mergers and acquisitions ("M&A"). The company earns revenue from two main sources: oil revenues, and other income, with oil revenues making up 97% of operating revenues.

Valeura usually targets mature plays or unconventional opportunities where it can leverage its existing infrastructure and operational expertise. This allows it to reduce finding costs relative to greenfield exploration projects. The organic growth that results from this portfolio generates strong cash flows which are used in order to replace reserves and develop underexplored opportunities.

Cost-effective production is a feature of many mid-cap E&P companies. As an investor, it’s important to look for E&P firms with low breakeven levels that can sustain profitability even under moderate price stress. Valeura prides itself on its operational excellence, with the 2023 management discussion & analysis ("MD&A") noting that expertise comes from its “proven international team to maintain a relentless focus on operational efficiency and margins while also aspiring to be a responsible corporate citizen in everything it does”.

Finally, the company pursues inorganic growth opportunities in Southeast Asia, where its activities are geared toward “value and operationally accretive” (M&A) targets, with a priority given to those targets that can improve current or “near-term production and cash flow”. This is also typical of firms like Valeura, which seek to use M&A to expand reserves and improve scale, betting that the resulting operational synergies can help manage costs more effectively.

In addition, assuming that it can renew its licences, Valeura holds an operated, high working interest position in the Deep Gas Play in the Thrace Basin of Türkiye, which management believes will be able to deliver significant value in the longer term. A portion of this will be farmed out, so that the company can pursue the next phase of appraisal work.

Overall, the business model is capable of generating high returns in an environment of supportive commodity price, and if the company can sustain low-cost production. Although oil prices have fallen, it is true that natural gas spot prices have risen in the last year, from $1.73 per million BTU to $3.97 per million BTU, the company does not receive any meaningful natural gas revenues as yet.

Tariffs Loom Large

Although hydrocarbons are not affected by the U.S.’s tariffs on Canada, the demand destruction that both the United States and Canada will suffer will result in an erosion in hydrocarbon prices. Oil has been the first casualty. If Canada enters into a recession, which many economists believe is highly likely, demand for hydrocarbons will fall. In addition, the tariffs imposed on the U.S. by Canada will lead to higher input costs for the company, reducing margins and operational efficiency. This will be especially true if the company cannot pass on costs to customers. There is evidence that the pass-through rate may be as high as 95% in the first year, but that it falls as customers shift to cheaper goods. If this is so, although markets may be pricing down Valeura too quickly, margins are likely to fall after a year or less.

The uncertainty from the tariff negotiations also act as a disincentive to invest. Valeura itself will probably have to hold off on its expansion, because it cannot correctly calculate future costs. The shape of US-Canada tariffs is still being negotiated. So, basically, it has become impossible to know what margins will look like next year.

As a result of tariffs, the company may also suffer from supply chain disruptions that result in delays in its capital projects, which would affect planned production ramp-ups, if the company decided that it could keep going despite uncertainty.

The most immediate impact of tariffs has been the decline in oil prices, which means that planned projects will now have reduced potential revenues. Margins since Liberation Day will likely be squeezed, and the market is probably anticipating this: as oil prices fall and costs increase as a consequence of tariffs, net operating margins will suffer, placing a lot of importance on Valeura’s effective use of hedging strategies. As cash flows fall, discretionary capital spending, which is critical for both maintaining production and future growth, will probably have to be deferred, potentially stalling expansion plans. This probably means that its Southeast Asia expansion will be the most affected. If you consider that the interest will effectively decline as investors become more risk averse, Southeast Asia’s attractions may decline, or, in the least, the investable universe will narrow. This is, at this point, speculation, but it is important speculation because the market’s abandoning of the stock suggests that it feels that profitability is getting away from the country.

Profitability Is Likely to Fall

Valeura’s net income has risen from -$19.53 million in 2020 to $240.8 million in 2024. The company first achieved profitability in 2023. The company’s EBITDA margin has also risen from -14.21% in 2020 to a record 58.79% in 2024. Such healthy EBITDA margins are important cushions against oil price downturns, although falling prices and tariff-induced costs will pressure margins. Valeura may need some degree of operational realignment to maintain margins or at least keep them at a breakeven level.

if the company sells 8,349 million barrels (mbbl) of oil in 2025, the same amount it sold in 2024, at a price of $60 per barrel, the company’s oil revenues will fall from $678.8 million in 2024 to $500.9 million, a fall of 26.2%. The company would need significant operational efficiency improvement to maintain current net income levels. In the likely event that Valeura does not significantly improve operational efficiency, then, investors should expect net income to fall by a similar amount to oil revenues, and this means that the value of the business also falls.

It does help that the company has no long-term debt, which gives it a lot of financial flexibility and resilience in a harsher macroeconomic environment. The company also has a lot of liquidity: its cash and cash equivalents, at $238.3 million, are nearly 28% of the Valeura’s total assets. This supports operational flexibility. Companies with sufficient cash are better positioned to continue investing, or even acquire distressed assets, when markets decline further.

Dr. Sean Guest, the company’s President and CEO, commented during the Q1 2025 earnings presentation, that,

“Our strong operational and financial performance continued throughout Q1 2025, and our business is more resilient than ever. With our corporate restructuring completed in November 2024, and the final tax payment under the previous structure now behind us, we see an energised ability to generate cash flow as we look at the remainder of 2025. We are carefully monitoring the current volatile market conditions while simultaneously reviewing and optimising our expenditures. However, our strong financial position with cash of US$238 million and no debt makes Valeura not only resilient, but also well positioned for attractive inorganic opportunities that may emerge during such a turbulent market environment. Notwithstanding the recent market volatility, we are maintaining all of our previously disclosed guidance assumptions for the year.”

Management seems confident about its ability to get through this period. I think, while the company will likely remain profitable and will remain a going concern, it is hard to say that it has not weakened.

It does help that Valeura has over $400 million in tax-deductible losses that it has accumulated over the years. The company has taken advantage of this by restructuring its operations so that three of its four producing assets will not have to pay taxes. In an investor conference last year, the CEO guided for $200 million in free cash flow ("FCF"). If you consider that its Thai oil income tax is 50% and that the gross savings are about $200 million, then, you can see how the company is probably stronger than investors believe.

Valuation

With $238.3 million in cash, the company’s cash and cash equivalents are worth 48.5% of its market capitalization. Its PE multiple is a dirt cheap 2.21. Assuming comparability, the iShares U.S. Oil & Gas Exploration & Production ETF (IEO) has a mean PE multiple of 12.44. If we slash 2025 net income by 30%, and assume an upward revision of the price in line with the US E&P PE multiple, the price would reach $19.85 per share, an upside of 328.8%. In the past, the company has discussed its valuation, arguing that it traded at half the peer group trading multiple.

That discount to peers seems to have grown even wider as the share price has cratered.

Conclusion

Investors should expect the price to keep on falling for some time, and that profitability will be severely hit in the next year or two. However, for long-term oriented investors, Valeura presents a compelling case because, at its current valuation, all sorts of severe risks have been baked in. There is a lot of margin of safety here. The risks of continuous price declines are such that, however, I give the company a Hold rating until there is some kind of market bottom.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Coinbase: A Company Based Purely On The Popularity Of Crypto
data.description:
Today I want to take a look at Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN), an online platform where users can trade, send and invest into cryptocurrencies. I have to rate the company as a HOLD due to their heavy reliance on cryptocurrencies. The company is, in my eyes, undervalued, but its reliance on the use of crypto such as Bitcoin, Ethereum and others makes it, in my eyes, volatile and a latter choice to an investment into a cryptocurrency itself, such as Bitcoin. To understand why I rate the company as a HOLD, we will first go through the market analysis, to then look at the 9 Pillars and finally calculate the company's intrinsic value using two of our intrinsic valuation models.

Introduction

Over the past few years, cryptocurrencies have been gaining in popularity, and thanks to that, companies like Coinbase have gained a huge customer base. Before getting to the positives, let me highlight the issue I have with Coinbase, and that is its pure reliance on cryptocurrencies. Personally, to me the main danger lies in the fact that more than 95% of their revenue comes from transaction fees, with 32% accounting for Bitcoin transactions.

Analysis

Market overview

With this in mind, we can deduce that most of their revenue relies on how many cryptocurrencies are being traded. If there is a year when crypto is battered, Coinbase will have a really hard time; on the other hand, if there is a year when crypto is praised, Coinbase will prosper as well. What I am trying to say is that mostly, in my eyes, the cash that flows through the company is reliant on crypto, and therefore, so is their stock price.

To zoom in on this, Bitcoin currently holds 59.85% of all money invested in crypto; therefore, in my opinion, it is quite safe to say that investing into Coinbase is very similar to investing into Bitcoin, except Coinbase is a company and Bitcoin is a cryptocurrency.

But leaving my thoughts behind, let's take a look at some positives. Overall, in 2024, the cryptocurrency market was valued at $2.49B and is expected to grow at a CAGR of 9.70% over the next 10 years, reaching a value of $6.29B in 2033.

A further thing I want to point out is that Trump has proposed, quite a few times, that the US should have a Bitcoin reserve, similarly to, for example, the Strategic Petroleum Reserve ("SPR"). He has also suggested that the US should invest in crypto overall. This would undoubtedly increase the value of all cryptocurrencies, which would in turn drive sales for Coinbase, also raising its value. But again, if that were to happen, it might be better if one invested specifically in Bitcoin rather than Coinbase which is reliant on it.

Nevertheless, now that we have an understanding of what is going on with the market, let's take a look at the company's fundamentals through the 9 Pillar Analysis.

9 Pillar Analysis

If you are new to the 9 Pillars, let me quickly explain what they are: Pillar 1 and 2 are the 5Y PE and P/FCF ratios, respectively. Pillars 3, 4, and 5 depict the 5Y revenue, net income, and FCF growth, with pillar 6 showing the 5-year profit margin and pillar 7 showing the change in shares outstanding. Pillar 8 shows the dividend growth, and Pillar 9 calculates the LTL/5Y FCF - Dividends ratio, which helps determine a company's ability to pay down all their debt within a safe period using their FCF while paying out dividends. Today we won't go through pillar 8, as Coinbase currently doesn't pay out any dividends. Nevertheless, below you can see that the company gets a rating of 4/8, so let's dive right in.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4 and 5)

Regarding the company's revenue, that has grown from $1.28B in 2020, to $6.29B in 2024, which accounts for an adjusted CAGR of 31.30%, which is rather high.

The company's net income has also grown by a lot, from $322.32M in 2020, to $2.58B in 2024, accounting for an adjusted CAGR of 22.40%, which is lower than the revenue CAGR, but still really good.

And finally, looking at the FCF, that has increased from $283.64M in 2020, to $2.56B in 2024, again, accounting for a high adjusted CAGR of 17.40%.

Now what I want to point out is that from 2020, bitcoin itself is up by almost 1,000%, while on average, the financials of Coinbase are up by only 623.96%. And don't take me wrong here; that is a great achievement. What I'm trying to point out is that if investing in Coinbase and Bitcoin carries similar risks, but one is based on the other, why not invest in the one that is its own thing.

Share Repurchases (Pillar 7)

Over the past 5 years, Coinbase has increased their shares outstanding by 200.00%, from 91.00M to 273.00M, which has in the long term heavily reduced the share value. And although this is something I don't like when a company does, it is understandable as Coinbase has gone public no more than a decade ago, and so in my eyes they still have the right to raise capital by selling out a large number of their shares.

Debt (Pillar 9)

Now one thing that I really don't like is that alongside selling out shares, Coinbase has been taking on a lot of debt. The company has gone from $83.00M in LTL in 2020 to $4.32B in LTL in 2024. Currently, they generate enough FCF to pay off all this debt in about 6 years, so things aren't that bad, but taking into account that a large portion of their FCF comes from selling out shares, combined with the increasing LTL, in my eyes isn't good for the long-term growth of the company, especially taking into account that they are to an extent cyclical, based on the popularity of cryptocurrencies.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Now, before moving to calculating the intrinsic value, I want to take a look at the PE and P/FCF ratios.

Starting with the 5Y PE, that is sitting at 42.49, but to this day it has actually decreased by quite a lot to now sit at only 16.59, which in my eyes shows undervaluation. Regarding the P/FCF, that is looking very similar to the PE, with the 5Y P/FCF sitting in a negative and the current P/FCF sitting nice and low at only 16.82. So overall, the company does seem undervalued compared to the net income and FCF they bring in. But let's figure this out with certainty through the Graham's Formula and the DCF model.

Intrinsic Valuation

First looking to the Graham's Formula, we will take the 5Y EPS sitting at $6.13, and based on the historical EPS growth, predict the future 5Y EPS CAGR at 20.33%. After combining these to values, we will get an intrinsic value of $218.53, 38.94% higher than the current trading price of $157.28.

Regarding the second valuation model, the DCF model, we will plug in the FCF from 2024, sitting at $2.56B, and based off of that, calculate the future FCF using a 10Y + CAGR of 8.30%. After that, I will discount all of that FFCF at a rate of 10.33% and get an intrinsic value of $241.06.

Finally, to calculate the ultimate intrinsic value, we will average out the two values we just calculated and get $229.80, 46.11% higher than the current trading price of $157.28.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I want to clarify that the only reason why I rate Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN) as a HOLD is because I find it much more safe, and profitable to invest in Bitcoin itself, as that is what fuels the growth of Coinbase. If the company's revenue wasn't based so much on transaction fees, my rating, due to the current undervaluation and great financial growth, would probably stand as a BUY. With that, If you are a believer in crypto, I don't see a reason to pick Coinbase over Bitcoin.

data.title: Saint Gobain - Inorganic Margin Increases Justify Price Target Increase (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

It's been about three months since my last coverage of Saint-Gobain (OTCPK:CODGF). This BBB+-rated building materials company is nothing to sneeze at - at least, that is the case at the right valuation. Now, in my last article, I gave the company a "Hold" rating due to high valuation. The company then proceeded to move higher following good results - but then proceeded to take a bit of a nosedive in conjunction with the tariff trends that we have been seeing.

In less than a month, it fell from over €100/share native to less than €82/share native. You might, therefore, think that the 2024 results, which I will be reviewing here, will justify my ongoing PT.

The fact is, however, I will be delivering a PT increase for Saint-Gobain on the back of some very impressive trends in terms of margins and upside - and that's what this article will focus on.

I still hold a small position in the company, but 90% of the shares I held were sold when the company bounced over €95/share, which was my "rotation" target previously.

Now, let's see what the annual results dictate that we do.

Saint Gobain - The company's results were better than expected

Results came out about 1.5 months ago at the time of writing this, but the catalyst for writing this article was the massive recent drop in share price, justifying an update in coverage on this company. It's no secret that Saint-Gobain is In the process of streamlining its portfolio. Like any major business in this field, it has lower-margin and underperforming non-core segments. The company's current plan is to divest those, and in doing so, fund acquisitions and mergers with higher-margin business units and companies across the materials/construction space, including the attractive construction chemicals business.

The 2024 results came in at a 3% EPS growth, which was double-digit above the forecast consensus. That's one of the reasons I'm updating and upgrading my targets here. Organic revenue was down for the year, with a sequential improvement in 2H, but much of this has to do with divestment effects.

What I want to focus on instead is the margin increase and company guidance to increase that margin further and remain above 11%. The company has gone ahead and made four strategic M&A's in the last year, spending well over €5B. It's not unfair to say that the company's overall composition is changing here.

As you can see, the focus here is not expanding into the USA more, but rather into EMEA and APAC, which I consider to be growth markets as well, much like company management. It's why I've slowly tilted away my portfolio from one geography/set of geographies and focused it more on companies that certainly are not strictly based there but that work with significant market presence there.

I don't need to mince words here because 2the 024 results were a record on several levels. The company's operating margin? Record at 11.4%. The company's net income? Record, €3.5B. The company's FCF? Record at €4B.

The most impressive part about the improvement seen by Saint-Gobain was the improvement in margin despite an increasingly difficult environment in its home geography, Europe. The company saw increased or stable margins in all segments here, in part because of its M&A's and divestment. Another factor that would impact against this improvement is volume pressure and inflation. The price-cost spread is positive for the company, and the company managed a full reduction of a day in OWCR in terms of cash flow. This represents, in €4B in FCF, a full 3x increase in cash generation since the transformation to what the company describes as a "deep-rooted cash culture" (Source)

And I like what I am seeing. Despite the company being a €20B market cap business, the leverage for Saint Gobain is less than 1.5x, which is one of the lowest in the entire segment. The strong financial discipline used by the company is also recognized, reflected in BBB+ in a journey to A-.

Sustainable construction is here to stay, despite what some political directions might be. The company is fully committed to this trend, and the ongoing implementation of various programmes is supporting this and Saint-Gobain.

While much of the company's M&A's are going to non-NA projects and companies, the investment CapEx for the company is actually increasingly US-focused, with several new projects to support the ongoing need for renovation and new building of houses and infrastructure in NA.

I would therefore characterize Saint-Gobain as well-positioned for future growth in NA as well and positioned well to defend against the impact of tariffs here, as much as it can be. However, the true advantage, I believe, comes from the emerging market focus of Saint Gobain.

The company has been reinforcing its presence in these markets for several years at this point, with significant construction ongoing in these economies. I believe this is the safest way to invest in this massive growth.

What growth, you ask?

We can point at 37 cities under construction in Egypt, 14 projects in Saudi Arabia alone, an Indian infrastructure program valued at almost €160B - with India actually being one of SGO's largest growth markets. The company's sales in the nation have increased by 100% in six years, and it's the third-largest contributor to group profit. Furthermore, SGO has started 27 new plants in India over the past six years as well.

In its home market, the company has a superb position as well, good potential for growth.

On the rotation side, SGO has sold nearly €10B worth of underperforming business units, with €6.8B worth of sales created from in-turn value-accretive acquisitions that the company has brought in, at a 20% EBITDA/sales, exchanging from a 5% EBITDA/Sales from the divested companies.

Shareholder returns in SGO are an attractive combination of buybacks and dividends, and over the past four years, it has delivered €5.6B of shareholder returns in the form of dividends and buybacks. SGO has increased its dividend by 5%, to €2.2/share, which comes to a current yield of 2.68%. Saint-Gobain has never been a high yielder, but this is an extremely attractive company in its own right without the dividend. The combination of buybacks, debt payments, and fundamental improvements spells out a very attractive shareholder return and yield. It's a bit diluted due to some debt activities recently, but the overall shareholder return is usually a very attractive one.

To be very clear, SGO is not the cheapest or highest-upside company available at this time - which I will also show you when I present the peers we have here. But it's a solid choice, a fair-value company at my PT increase. The company's upside includes regulation-related factors where the company's products increase energy efficiency, and the divestment of low-margin businesses is extremely positive for the company's bottom line, especially in combination with high-margin M&A's. Examples of these that I am including in my forecast are Applied Technologies, Chryso, and Fosroc, among others.

Negatives do exist for the company. Political uncertainty and low overall business confidence in the home market are persisting negatives and headwinds, and the volume declines have not been stopped. Also, the company's construction chemicals business is still "half-baked" compared to some of the competitors - SGO needs to expand here. Those are the primary risks that is see here.

Let's look at pricing and upside.

Saint-Gobain - We expect an upside with better margins; I say a €82/share PT is now valid.

I raised my PT in the last article as well but failed to fully take into account the effects of margin increase and the top-line growth potential in emerging markets. That is what I am correcting in this article. The buy margin here is low - as I am writing, the company is trading around €1/share below my proposed PT - but it nonetheless represents a "BUY".

Saint-Gobain has typically traded at 11-13x. I'm willing to raise the range to 14-15x P/E now, the justification being the margin increases and volume increases that are expected of the company. Current expectations put the company close to a 5-6% growth rate, which is in excess of the historical growth rate. At a 14-15x P/E, the company's RoR is over 16.5%, which is well within what I expect from a company such as this and also meets my investment criteria.

Now that the company has dropped quite a bit, I also believe it warrants a look and potential investment if you believe that this trend may hold. My PT has been increased by around 10% since my last article due to higher clarity in the higher-margin business, which justifies a margin potential of up to 12%, thereby moving higher in terms of EPS. The Margin of 12-12.5% that I believe possible here is in excess of management guidance, and that is why I am positive. This also comes with the potential for further buybacks, which the company has been doing liberally for years as it's been cheap. I don't see historical levels matching current ones, but I forecast a €300M buyback during this fiscal.

None of this takes away the volatility that does exist in the stock overall, but I believe the upside accounts for this well enough. Forecast-wise, I don't see the potential for a heavy downside after the superb performance this year, and likely superb performance going forward as well. That most of the revenue is cyclical only means that we can't forecast it above 16-18x P/E, which I have specifically not done. Instead, I point to how the company uses available capital, optimizes its portfolio, and rewards shareholders for value justification.

Aside from historicals, we have peer averages. The company's closest competitors or peers are typically considered to be other building materials businesses. These include companies like Sika AG (OTCPK:SXYAY), CRH, Masco, and others. Common for these companies is trading at relatively cyclical valuations, going up and down. Out of its peer group, only CRH is larger, but CRH is also more "one-dimensional" in terms of its product lineup. My choice in the sector, aside from turning SGO into a "BUY", is actually Swiss company Sika AG. The company has dropped quite a bit, is now trading at below 200 CHF per share, which allows for an upside and a yield of above 1.8%. It does not make SGO unattractive in the least, but it is an alternative I would look at.

Common for these building materials giants is that they all have very low yield. SGO is in fact the highest at above 2.5%, and that's worth considering as well.

CODGF is the ADR for SGO, and it's your standard 1:1 Share ADR, meaning my €82/share Price target comes to $93/share. As I am writing this, the ADR is at $92.5, which means that just like the native, it's barely a "BUY" here.

But it is a buy, and I am updating my thesis.

Thesis

  • My thesis for Saint-Gobain is generally a positive one - provided you can "BUY" the company at an attractive valuation, which I believe you now can do.
  • The company is a very solid building material company. It has world-leading operations across several continents and has demonstrated over time the ability to outperform its shareholders as well as outperform the market. It also has specific attractive business segments in the EMEA and APAC sectors, which I believe are likely to outperform.
  • I recently sold my shares after my last article prior to this one in 2024 and sold the remainder of the shares almost entirely when the company hit over €90/share.
  • I now upgrade SGO to a "BUY", and I say a PT of €82/share, upgraded as of this particular time. This update is as of April 2025. I am slowly starting to add SGO shares again.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €82/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of April 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UTEN: Trump's Interest In The 10-Year Treasury Yield
data.description:
One of the periods the 10-year Treasuries delivered consistently attractive risk-adjusted returns for their asset class was back in 2019-2020. Those two years were characterized by decreasing rate expectations, Fed easing, and a pandemic that prompted the central bank to drop the policy rate close to zero.

Evidently, a Sharpe ratio above 2 is possible if you hold 10-year Treasury notes when the wind is blowing so favorably. What you see above is the result of the rare combination of consistently falling yields and minimal volatility. Conditions that are not present yet.

The US Treasury 10 Year Note ETF (NASDAQ:UTEN), which I first covered here, offers exposure to that part of the yield curve. In my first article, I explained why I think short duration is preferable right now. While I maintain that thesis, a lot has transpired since then and investors who are dumping equities for 10-year Treasuries could enjoy an interesting tailwind that should not be underestimated.

One week ago, President Trump, in a Truth Social post, directly told the Fed Chairman to cut interest rates. Then, a few days ago, he doubled down by saying that the central bank should cut rates.

Everyone who can think critically will be led to the reasonable conclusion that Trump doesn't really expect that the Fed will cut rates because he asks them to. This is about controlling the narrative and may be serving multiple purposes.

First, his tariffs have hit equity markets hard as the growth scare intensified and shifting the blame to the Fed's indecision regarding the rate path makes sense if he intends to keep pushing. It indicates he doesn't intend to back down and may have more policies to introduce, which could hurt the economy, at least in the short term.

Moreover, introducing the narrative about the Fed being resistant to calls for lower rates and being responsible for growth challenges can justify fiscal loosening and more aggressive tariff policies in the future.

Last, he could also simply want to influence the markets to price in a higher probability for an early rate cut this year and drive yields down through that mechanism. It's not a secret that he wants the 10-Year Treasury rate to fall. Two months ago, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent stated that he and Trump are "focused on the 10-year Treasury", rather than the Fed Funds rate.

And that is crucial for investors who have bet on that curve segment, as there are multiple ways the current administration can bring 10-year Treasury yields down. First, the Treasury can prioritize issuing T-Bills instead of notes and bonds to fund the deficit and alleviate supply pressure for 10-year Treasuries; this is the most obvious approach because Bessent has said that the auction sizes, which reflect a T-Bill focus, could be maintained for the foreseeable future. DOGE's efforts to cut the deficit are also consistent with the intention to lower yields because they allow them to go easier on note issuance.

Another approach, which Bessent has been transparent about, would be to lower energy prices. If inflation expectations decrease, the risk premium of 10-year Treasuries should narrow, bringing the yield down. Energy is an important component of inflation as it impacts not just headline CPI but core PCE through its influence on operational costs for service providers and products that rely on energy.

To lower energy prices, the administration would need to increase the oil supply. Trump has been encouraging the industry to boost oil drilling in the recent past. But U.S. frackers are now cautious because a lot of them drove themselves into bankruptcy by overdrilling in the last decade. Persuading the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries and Saudi Arabia to increase the oil supply may be Trump's only chance of achieving lower energy prices, but Saudi Arabia has already expressed an unwillingness to do so.

At the same time, however, Trump's reciprocal tariffs, which involve Saudi Arabia, could provide some leverage that didn't exist before. It's too early to know if and how Saudi Arabia will negotiate an exemption once the 90-day pause expires but this is an important part of the narrative investors should monitor.

Still, the more immediate risks of increased volatility and a Fed cornered into keeping its policy rate at the current level make me maintain my hold rating on UTEN. Short duration is still the more attractive option, as I recently explained here.

That being said, I do think that the current tariff narrative may soon reveal some interesting tailwinds for the 10-Year Treasury yield and headwinds for the US dollar as the Trump administration seems keen on lowering the former and weakening the latter. It's possible that the currently unattractive risk/reward profile of UTEN and similar funds becomes less so in the next couple of months.

What's your view? Do you think that 10-Year Treasury yields will materially fall this year? Let me know in the comments.

Thank you for your attention.
